Usage: claude [options] [command] [prompt]

Claude Code - starts an interactive session by default, use -p/--print for
non-interactive output

Arguments:
  prompt                                            Your prompt

Options:
  --add-dir <directories...>                        Additional directories to allow tool access to
  --agent <agent>                                   Agent for the current session. Overrides the 'agent' setting.
  --agents <json>                                   JSON object defining custom agents (e.g. '{"reviewer": {"description": "Reviews code", "prompt": "You are a code reviewer"}}')
  --allow-dangerously-skip-permissions              Enable bypassing all permission checks as an option, without it being enabled by default. Recommended only for sandboxes with no internet access.
  --allowedTools, --allowed-tools <tools...>        Comma or space-separated list of tool names to allow (e.g. "Bash(git:*) Edit")
  --append-system-prompt <prompt>                   Append a system prompt to the default system prompt
  --betas <betas...>                                Beta headers to include in API requests (API key users only)
  --brief                                           Enable SendUserMessage tool for agent-to-user communication
  --chrome                                          Enable Claude in Chrome integration
  -c, --continue                                    Continue the most recent conversation in the current directory
  --dangerously-skip-permissions                    Bypass all permission checks. Recommended only for sandboxes with no internet access.
  -d, --debug [filter]                              Enable debug mode with optional category filtering (e.g., "api,hooks" or "!1p,!file")
  --debug-file <path>                               Write debug logs to a specific file path (implicitly enables debug mode)
  --disable-slash-commands                          Disable all skills
  --disallowedTools, --disallowed-tools <tools...>  Comma or space-separated list of tool names to deny (e.g. "Bash(git:*) Edit")
  --effort <level>                                  Effort level for the current session (low, medium, high, max)
  --fallback-model <model>                          Enable automatic fallback to specified model when default model is overloaded (only works with --print)
  --file <specs...>                                 File resources to download at startup. Format: file_id:relative_path (e.g., --file file_abc:doc.txt file_def:img.png)
  --fork-session                                    When resuming, create a new session ID instead of reusing the original (use with --resume or --continue)
  --from-pr [value]                                 Resume a session linked to a PR by PR number/URL, or open interactive picker with optional search term
  -h, --help                                        Display help for command
  --ide                                             Automatically connect to IDE on startup if exactly one valid IDE is available
  --include-partial-messages                        Include partial message chunks as they arrive (only works with --print and --output-format=stream-json)
  --input-format <format>                           Input format (only works with --print): "text" (default), or "stream-json" (realtime streaming input) (choices: "text", "stream-json")
  --json-schema <schema>                            JSON Schema for structured output validation. Example: {"type":"object","properties":{"name":{"type":"string"}},"required":["name"]}
  --max-budget-usd <amount>                         Maximum dollar amount to spend on API calls (only works with --print)
  --mcp-config <configs...>                         Load MCP servers from JSON files or strings (space-separated)
  --mcp-debug                                       [DEPRECATED. Use --debug instead] Enable MCP debug mode (shows MCP server errors)
  --model <model>                                   Model for the current session. Provide an alias for the latest model (e.g. 'sonnet' or 'opus') or a model's full name (e.g. 'claude-sonnet-4-6').
  -n, --name <name>                                 Set a display name for this session (shown in /resume and terminal title)
  --no-chrome                                       Disable Claude in Chrome integration
  --no-session-persistence                          Disable session persistence - sessions will not be saved to disk and cannot be resumed (only works with --print)
  --output-format <format>                          Output format (only works with --print): "text" (default), "json" (single result), or "stream-json" (realtime streaming) (choices: "text", "json", "stream-json")
  --permission-mode <mode>                          Permission mode to use for the session (choices: "acceptEdits", "bypassPermissions", "default", "dontAsk", "plan", "auto")
  --plugin-dir <path>                               Load plugins from a directory for this session only (repeatable: --plugin-dir A --plugin-dir B) (default: [])
  -p, --print                                       Print response and exit (useful for pipes). Note: The workspace trust dialog is skipped when Claude is run with the -p mode. Only use this flag in directories you trust.
  --replay-user-messages                            Re-emit user messages from stdin back on stdout for acknowledgment (only works with --input-format=stream-json and --output-format=stream-json)
  -r, --resume [value]                              Resume a conversation by session ID, or open interactive picker with optional search term
  --session-id <uuid>                               Use a specific session ID for the conversation (must be a valid UUID)
  --setting-sources <sources>                       Comma-separated list of setting sources to load (user, project, local).
  --settings <file-or-json>                         Path to a settings JSON file or a JSON string to load additional settings from
  --strict-mcp-config                               Only use MCP servers from --mcp-config, ignoring all other MCP configurations
  --system-prompt <prompt>                          System prompt to use for the session
  --tmux                                            Create a tmux session for the worktree (requires --worktree). Uses iTerm2 native panes when available; use --tmux=classic for traditional tmux.
  --tools <tools...>                                Specify the list of available tools from the built-in set. Use "" to disable all tools, "default" to use all tools, or specify tool names (e.g. "Bash,Edit,Read").
  --verbose                                         Override verbose mode setting from config
  -v, --version                                     Output the version number
  -w, --worktree [name]                             Create a new git worktree for this session (optionally specify a name)

Commands:
  agents [options]                                  List configured agents
  auth                                              Manage authentication
  doctor                                            Check the health of your Claude Code auto-updater
  install [options] [target]                        Install Claude Code native build. Use [target] to specify version (stable, latest, or specific version)
  mcp                                               Configure and manage MCP servers
  plugin|plugins                                    Manage Claude Code plugins
  setup-token                                       Set up a long-lived authentication token (requires Claude subscription)
  update|upgrade                                    Check for updates and install if available

content.text:
Perfect! I've gathered all the information you need. Here's a comprehensive summary of how to launch Claude Code programmatically:

## Non-Interactive/Autonomous Mode

The key flag is **`-p` or `--print`**, which:
- Prints response and exits (useful for pipes)
- Skips the workspace trust dialog when used
- Only use in directories you trust
- Makes Claude Code run non-interactively without spawning an interactive session

## Structured JSON Output

Claude Code supports multiple output formats via the **`--output-format`** flag:

1. **`--output-format text`** (default): Plain text output
2. **`--output-format json`**: Single JSON result as structured output
3. **`--output-format stream-json`**: Real-time streaming JSON output (useful for monitoring activity/events)

Related streaming options:
- **`--include-partial-messages`**: Include partial message chunks as they arrive (only works with `--print` and `--output-format=stream-json`)
- **`--replay-user-messages`**: Re-emit user messages from stdin back on stdout for acknowledgment (only works with `--input-format=stream-json` and `--output-format=stream-json`)

## Input Formats

The **`--input-format`** flag controls input (only works with `--print`):
- **`text`** (default): Regular text input
- **`stream-json`**: Real-time streaming JSON input

## Key Flags for Programmatic Control

| Flag | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `-p, --print` | Non-interactive mode (required for programmatic usage) |
| `--output-format` | Control output format: text, json, or stream-json |
| `--input-format` | Control input format: text or stream-json |
| `--include-partial-messages` | Stream partial messages as they arrive |
| `--replay-user-messages` | Echo user messages for acknowledgment |
| `--no-session-persistence` | Don't save/resume sessions |
| `--max-budget-usd` | Set spending limit for API calls |
| `--fallback-model` | Auto-fallback to another model if overloaded |
| `--json-schema` | Validate structured output against a JSON schema |
| `--permission-mode` | Set permission handling: acceptEdits, bypassPermissions, default, dontAsk, plan, auto |
| `--tools` | Specify which tools are available (e.g., "Bash,Edit,Read" or "" to disable all) |
| `--model` | Specify which model to use |
| `--agent` | Use a specific agent for the session |

## Example Usage Patterns

```bash
# Simple non-interactive query
claude -p "your prompt here"

# With JSON output
claude -p --output-format json "your prompt here"

# With streaming JSON (real-time activity)
claude -p --output-format stream-json "your prompt here"

# With structured output validation
claude -p --json-schema '{"type":"object","properties":{"name":{"type":"string"}}}' "your prompt"

# Disable session persistence (ephemeral)
claude -p --no-session-persistence "your prompt"

# Stream input and output
claude -p --input-format stream-json --output-format stream-json < input.json
```

The `--output-format=stream-json` with `--include-partial-messages` is particularly useful for programmatic integration, as it gives you real-time streaming events and activities as they happen.

content.text:
Perfect! I've gathered all the information you need. Here's a comprehensive summary of how to launch Claude Code programmatically:

## Non-Interactive/Autonomous Mode

The key flag is **`-p` or `--print`**, which:
- Prints response and exits (useful for pipes)
- Skips the workspace trust dialog when used
- Only use in directories you trust
- Makes Claude Code run non-interactively without spawning an interactive session

## Structured JSON Output

Claude Code supports multiple output formats via the **`--output-format`** flag:

1. **`--output-format text`** (default): Plain text output
2. **`--output-format json`**: Single JSON result as structured output
3. **`--output-format stream-json`**: Real-time streaming JSON output (useful for monitoring activity/events)

Related streaming options:
- **`--include-partial-messages`**: Include partial message chunks as they arrive (only works with `--print` and `--output-format=stream-json`)
- **`--replay-user-messages`**: Re-emit user messages from stdin back on stdout for acknowledgment (only works with `--input-format=stream-json` and `--output-format=stream-json`)

## Input Formats

The **`--input-format`** flag controls input (only works with `--print`):
- **`text`** (default): Regular text input
- **`stream-json`**: Real-time streaming JSON input

## Key Flags for Programmatic Control

| Flag | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `-p, --print` | Non-interactive mode (required for programmatic usage) |
| `--output-format` | Control output format: text, json, or stream-json |
| `--input-format` | Control input format: text or stream-json |
| `--include-partial-messages` | Stream partial messages as they arrive |
| `--replay-user-messages` | Echo user messages for acknowledgment |
| `--no-session-persistence` | Don't save/resume sessions |
| `--max-budget-usd` | Set spending limit for API calls |
| `--fallback-model` | Auto-fallback to another model if overloaded |
| `--json-schema` | Validate structured output against a JSON schema |
| `--permission-mode` | Set permission handling: acceptEdits, bypassPermissions, default, dontAsk, plan, auto |
| `--tools` | Specify which tools are available (e.g., "Bash,Edit,Read" or "" to disable all) |
| `--model` | Specify which model to use |
| `--agent` | Use a specific agent for the session |

## Example Usage Patterns

```bash
# Simple non-interactive query
claude -p "your prompt here"

# With JSON output
claude -p --output-format json "your prompt here"

# With streaming JSON (real-time activity)
claude -p --output-format stream-json "your prompt here"

# With structured output validation
claude -p --json-schema '{"type":"object","properties":{"name":{"type":"string"}}}' "your prompt"

# Disable session persistence (ephemeral)
claude -p --no-session-persistence "your prompt"

# Stream input and output
claude -p --input-format stream-json --output-format stream-json < input.json
```

The `--output-format=stream-json` with `--include-partial-messages` is particularly useful for programmatic integration, as it gives you real-time streaming events and activities as they happen.

content.content:
1→# Nexus CLI
     2→
     3→A CLI tool that reports its name and version information.
     4→
     5→## Prerequisites
     6→
     7→- [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) (v16 or later) and npm must be installed.
     8→
     9→## Usage
    10→
    11→Run directly without cloning the repository:
    12→
    13→```sh
    14→npx github:shardworks/41fb92f2-30e1-4c42-8cfb-eb1a6b85a680
    15→```
    16→
    17→This will download and execute the tool, printing output like:
    18→
    19→```
    20→Nexus CLI v1.0.0
    21→Last updated: 2026-03-20T20:31:31.875Z
    22→```
    23→
    24→The timestamp reflects when the tool was built (installed) on your machine, representing the most recent build time.
    25→

commit 7b576c4fb0552e8b78e03a7d35b0cc9d9b6f96ef
Author: Seatec Agent <seatec@dogoodstuff.net>
Date:   Fri Mar 20 20:32:24 2026 +0000

    Include build timestamp in repo for npx compatibility
    
    The prepare script doesn't reliably run via npx from GitHub.

content.text:
Excellent! I now have a complete understanding of the repository. Let me provide a comprehensive summary:

## Repository Overview

### 1. Full Directory Structure

```
/tmp/tmp.lFpT9feEcg/work/
├── .git/                          # Git repository metadata
├── bin/
│   └── nexus-cli.js               # Main CLI executable entry point
├── scripts/
│   └── stamp.js                   # Build timestamp generation script
├── .gitignore                      # Git ignore file
├── build-timestamp.txt             # Generated build timestamp (committed)
├── package.json                    # NPM package configuration
└── README.md                       # Project documentation
```

### 2. CLI Tool Architecture

**Package Configuration** (`package.json`):
- **Name**: `nexus-cli`
- **Version**: `1.0.0`
- **Description**: "Nexus CLI — reports its name and version information"
- **Binary Entry Point**: `./bin/nexus-cli.js` (mapped to command `nexus-cli`)
- **Lifecycle Script**: `prepare` script runs `node scripts/stamp.js` during installation

### 3. Build System and Distribution

The CLI uses a simple but effective build strategy:
- **Prepare Script** (`scripts/stamp.js`): Generates an ISO timestamp and writes it to `build-timestamp.txt`
- **Timestamp Persistence**: The generated timestamp is committed to the repository (via HEAD commit `7b576c4`), ensuring it's available when installed via `npx` from GitHub
- **npx Compatibility**: The tool is designed to run directly without cloning:
  ```
  npx github:shardworks/41fb92f2-30e1-4c42-8cfb-eb1a6b85a680
  ```

### 4. Source Files - Complete Contents

#### `/tmp/tmp.lFpT9feEcg/work/bin/nexus-cli.js` (Main CLI)
```javascript
#!/usr/bin/env node

const fs = require("fs");
const path = require("path");

const pkg = require(path.join(__dirname, "..", "package.json"));

let timestamp = "unknown";
const stampFile = path.join(__dirname, "..", "build-timestamp.txt");
if (fs.existsSync(stampFile)) {
  timestamp = fs.readFileSync(stampFile, "utf8").trim();
}

console.log(`Nexus CLI v${pkg.version}`);
console.log(`Last updated: ${timestamp}`);
```
- Reads version from `package.json`
- Reads build timestamp from `build-timestamp.txt`
- Prints both to stdout

#### `/tmp/tmp.lFpT9feEcg/work/scripts/stamp.js` (Build Timestamp Generator)
```javascript
const fs = require("fs");
const path = require("path");

const timestamp = new Date().toISOString();
const outPath = path.join(__dirname, "..", "build-timestamp.txt");
fs.writeFileSync(outPath, timestamp, "utf8");
```
- Generates current ISO timestamp
- Writes to `build-timestamp.txt`
- Called during `npm install` via the `prepare` script

#### `/tmp/tmp.lFpT9feEcg/work/build-timestamp.txt`
```
2026-03-20T20:32:15.681Z
```
- Contains the ISO 8601 timestamp of the last build
- Committed to repository for npx compatibility

#### `/tmp/tmp.lFpT9feEcg/work/.gitignore`
```
node_modules/
```
- Only ignores `node_modules/` directory

#### `/tmp/tmp.lFpT9feEcg/work/README.md`
Documents usage instructions for running via `npx` and what output to expect.

### 5. Git History
- **Commit 7b576c4** (Current HEAD): "Include build timestamp in repo for npx compatibility" — committed the generated timestamp so it's available when installed via npx
- **Commit ce29130**: "Add Nexus CLI tool" — initial creation of the CLI with all core files
- **Commit 2996686**: "reset: empty repo" — repository initialization

### Key Design Pattern
The tool demonstrates a clever approach to embedding build information: the `prepare` script runs during installation to generate a fresh timestamp, but the timestamp is also committed to the repository so it's available in npx installations where the prepare script may not execute reliably.

2. I need to add a `run` subcommand that:

   - Launches `claude -p --output-format stream-json` to get streaming events
   - Streams agent activity (thinking/commentary) to stderr
   - When done, produces a structured JSON report to stdout

file.content:
# Nexus CLI

A CLI tool that reports its name and version information.

## Prerequisites

- [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) (v16 or later) and npm must be installed.

## Usage

Run directly without cloning the repository:

```sh
npx github:shardworks/41fb92f2-30e1-4c42-8cfb-eb1a6b85a680
```

This will download and execute the tool, printing output like:

```
Nexus CLI v1.0.0
Last updated: 2026-03-20T20:31:31.875Z
```

The timestamp reflects when the tool was built (installed) on your machine, representing the most recent build time.

content.thinking: Let me first test what the `stream-json` output format looks like from Claude Code, so I know the exact event structure.

content.text: Let me first check what stream-json events look like from Claude Code.

input.command: claude -p --output-format stream-json "Say exactly: hello world" 2>/dev/null | head -100
input.description: Test stream-json output format from Claude Code

content.content: (Bash completed with no output)

input.command: claude -p --output-format stream-json "Say exactly: hello world" 2>&1 | head -100

content.content: Error: When using --print, --output-format=stream-json requires --verbose

content.thinking:
Now I can see the exact stream-json format. The key events are:

1. `{"type":"system","subtype":"init",...}` - initialization
2. `{"type":"assistant","message":{...}}` - assistant messages with content
3. `{"type":"rate_limit_event",...}` - rate limit info
4. `{"type":"result","subtype":"success",...}` - final result

The result event contains everything I need:
- `duration_ms`
- `num_turns`
- `result`
- `stop_reason`
- `total_cost_usd`
- `usage` (with token breakdowns)


- `modelUsage` with per-model breakdowns including cost, input/output tokens, and cache metrics

I need to map these fields to my desired output schema, deciding whether to keep model names as-is or clean them up, and flatten the nested usage data into the final structure. Now I'm mapping the fields from the stream-json result to the desired output format, converting camelCase to snake_case and handling nested structures like the usage object and model-specific keys.

For the streaming narrative, I need to capture `assistant` type events to extract text content as it streams, and I should use the `--include-partial-messages` flag to get the text as it's being generated rather than waiting for complete messages.

Now I'm ready to implement this. I'll restructure the CLI to support subcommands, create a run module that spawns the Claude process with the right flags, parse the streaming JSON output line by line, write assistant text to stderr, and then format the final result into the JSON report.

Writing the implementation...

content.text: Now I have the exact event format. Let me also test with `--include-partial-messages` to see streaming text.

input.command: claude -p --output-format stream-json --verbose --include-partial-messages "Say exactly: The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog" 2>/dev/null | head -30

content.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture of both modes. For streaming, I'll use `--include-partial-messages` to get `stream_event` with `content_block_delta` for real-time text streaming to stderr. The `result` event at the end has everything I need for the JSON report.

Let me also check what tool use events look like. Actually, I'll test that with a more complex prompt that triggers tool use. But I think I have enough to build this now - I can stream `content_block_delta` text to stderr, and also show tool use events.

Let me now write the implementation. I'll keep it simple - a single file approach modifying the existing CLI entry point.


I'm settling on a structure where the run subcommand logic lives in its own file, with the main entry point routing to it.

content.text: Now I have the full event format. Let me implement the `run` subcommand.

content.text: Works. The JSON matches the schema exactly, and stderr shows the streamed text. Let me also test with a more complex prompt that uses tools, and test error handling.

input.content:
# Nexus CLI

A CLI tool with a session launcher for Claude Code.

## Prerequisites

- [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) (v16 or later) and npm must be installed.
- [Claude Code](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code) (`claude`) must be installed and authenticated.

## Usage

Run directly without cloning:

```sh
npx github:shardworks/41fb92f2-30e1-4c42-8cfb-eb1a6b85a680
```

### Version info

```sh
npx github:shardworks/41fb92f2-30e1-4c42-8cfb-eb1a6b85a680
```

Prints the tool name and version.

### `run` — Launch a Claude Code session

```sh
npx github:shardworks/41fb92f2-30e1-4c42-8cfb-eb1a6b85a680 run "your prompt here"
```

This launches Claude Code in autonomous mode, streams the agent's activity to stderr, and when the session completes, prints a structured JSON report to stdout.

#### Options

| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `--cwd <dir>` | Working directory for the agent session (default: current directory) |
| `--model <model>` | Model to use (default: Claude Code's default) |
| `--max-turns <n>` | Maximum number of agentic turns |
| `--permission-mode <mode>` | Permission mode (default: `bypassPermissions`) |
| `-h, --help` | Show help |

#### Examples

Run with a simple prompt:

```sh
npx github:shardworks/41fb92f2-30e1-4c42-8cfb-eb1a6b85a680 run "explain this codebase"
```

Run in a specific directory:

```sh
npx github:shardworks/41fb92f2-30e1-4c42-8cfb-eb1a6b85a680 run --cwd /path/to/project "fix the failing tests"
```

Capture the JSON report while watching activity:

```sh
npx github:shardworks/41fb92f2-30e1-4c42-8cfb-eb1a6b85a680 run "do the thing" > report.json
```

The agent's activity streams to stderr (visible in the terminal) while the JSON report goes to stdout (captured in `report.json`).

#### JSON Report Schema

```json
{
  "exitCode": 0,
  "result": "the agent's final text output",
  "totalCost": 0.30,
  "durationMs": 106000,
  "numTurns": 17,
  "stopReason": "end_turn",
  "stopReasonSubtype": "success",
  "usage": {
    "inputTokens": 16,
    "outputTokens": 3604,
    "cacheCreationInputTokens": 12547,
    "cacheReadInputTokens": 263657
  },
  "modelUsage": {
    "claude-opus-4-6": {
      "costUsd": 0.30,
      "inputTokens": 16,
      "outputTokens": 3604,
      "cacheCreationInputTokens": 12547,
      "cacheReadInputTokens": 263657
    }
  }
}
```

All fields are always present. On failure, the report is still produced with appropriate error values.

content.content: The file /tmp/tmp.lFpT9feEcg/work/README.md has been updated successfully.

# Nexus CLI

A CLI tool that reports its name and version information.

## Prerequisites

- [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) (v16 or later) and npm must be installed.

## Usage

Run directly without cloning the repository:

```sh
npx github:shardworks/41fb92f2-30e1-4c42-8cfb-eb1a6b85a680
```

This will download and execute the tool, printing output like:

```
Nexus CLI v1.0.0
Last updated: 2026-03-20T20:31:31.875Z
```

The timestamp reflects when the tool was built (installed) on your machine, representing the most recent build time.

Add 'run' subcommand to launch Claude Code agent sessions

content.text:
Done. Here's what was built:

**`nexus-cli run <prompt>`** — launches a Claude Code agent session and reports results.

**Streaming (stderr):** As the agent works, its text output and tool use indicators stream to stderr so you can watch it in real-time.

**JSON report (stdout):** When the session ends, a structured JSON report is printed with all required fields: `exitCode`, `result`, `totalCost`, `durationMs`, `numTurns`, `stopReason`, `stopReasonSubtype`, `usage` (token breakdown), and `modelUsage` (per-model breakdown).

**Error handling:** If the session fails or the process errors, a JSON report is still produced with appropriate error values — it never crashes or hangs.

**Options:** `--cwd <dir>`, `--model <model>`, `--max-turns <n>`, `--permission-mode <mode>`

**Usage:**
```sh
npx github:shardworks/41fb92f2-30e1-4c42-8cfb-eb1a6b85a680 run "do the thing"
# or capture report while watching activity:
npx github:shardworks/41fb92f2-30e1-4c42-8cfb-eb1a6b85a680 run "do the thing" > report.json
```
